title-group.article-title: Phytochemical composition and anti-Alzheimer potential of medicinal plants from Central and West Africa: Systematic review

contrib-group.aff: aDepartment of General Medicine, School of Medicine and Pharmacy, University of Rwanda, Rwanda
bDepartment of Human Anatomy, Faculty of Biomedical Sciences, Kampala International University, Western campus, Uganda
cDepartment of Dental Surgery, School of Medicine and Pharmacy, University of Rwanda, Rwanda
dDepartment of Human Anatomy, School of Medicine and Pharmacy, University of Rwanda, Rwanda
eDepartment of Nursing Sciences, Kampala International University, Western campus, Uganda
fDepartment of Human Physiology, School of Medicine and Pharmacy, University of Rwanda, Rwanda

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).

abstract.p: In recent time, the use of medicinal plants in the management of human disease conditions has gained immense attention. The aim of the present review was to explore the phytochemical composition and anti-Alzheimer potential of medicinal plants from Central and West Africa. A structured systematic approach was used to conduct the present systematic review of articles. Scopus, PubMed, and Web of Science were searched using the following search terms combined by Boolean operators: (“Alzheimer’s disease” OR “Neurodegenerative disease” OR "Nervous system diseases") AND (“Extract” OR "herbs" OR “Plant”) AND (“West Africa” OR “Central Africa” OR “Africa”) on the 6th of July 2024 without any filters. Following the conclusion of the title, abstract, and full text screening, only 13 articles were included. Most of the included studies (10/13, 77 %) were conducted between 2014 and 2024. Geographically, 9 (69 %) studies were conducted in Nigeria. Plants identified in the present study include: Solanum macrocarpon, Solanum nigru, Pteleopsis suberosa, Macrosphyra longistyl, Beta vulgaris, Persea americana, Syzygium aromaticum, Citrullus lanatus, Cucumeropsis mannii, Lagenaria siceraria, Achyranthes aspera Linn., Tithonia diversifolia. These plants were found to contain Luteolin, Catechin, Decanoic acid methyl ester, 11,14-Eicosadienoic acid methyl ester, Caffeic acid, Syringic acid, Azelaic acid. The plant-derived phytochemicals were reported to modulate critical Alzheimer’s disease (AD) pathways, notably oxidative stress, acetylcholinesterase (AChE) inhibition, and neuroinflammation. In conclusion, Central and West African medicinal plants represent a rich reservoir of multifunctional neuroprotective metabolites capable of targeting diverse AD pathways.

list-item.p: Plant from Central and West Africa contains Luteolin, Catechin, Decanoic acid methyl ester.
Plant-derived phytochemicals modulated oxidative stress pathway.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Alzheimer's disease (AD) is a neurodegenerative disease marked by progressive disabling. Neurodegenerative activities lead to memory loss, cognitive disorder, synapse and cell dysfunction, oxidative stress, neuroinflammation, and dysfunctional neurotransmission, especially in the cholinergic systems [1], [2]. Alzheimer's disease is characterized by accumulation of intracellular neuro-fibrillary tangles and extracellular amyloid plaques in the brain, and the degeneration of neurons in different regions [3]. Biochemically, the acetylcholine levels are reduced. At the clinical level, there is demonstration of disruptive behavioral changes, personality changes, and progressive loss of memory [4]. The prevalence of AD among the aged is on the increase across the globe and posing a growing burden on health care systems, caregivers and society. Without curative treatments, most studies have been directed to slow or prevent progression of the disease, alleviate the symptoms and identification of new therapeutic agents [3], [4]. The use of traditional medicinal plants provides an immense source of bioactive compounds that would be relevant in these endeavors.

In Africa, especially in Central and West Africa, folk healers have traditionally employed a remarkable variety of medicinal plants to treat the symptoms of cognitive impairment, memory lapse, mental exhaustion, and other conditions that can be complicated by neurological impairment. However, a large portion of such traditional knowledge has not been studied in serious phytochemical and pharmacological research in AD-relevant pathways. Empirical evidence has already confirmed that multiple plant species in Africa contain phytochemicals with acetylcholinesterase inhibitory activity and powerful antioxidant potential: e.g. luteolin, zingerone and rutin. Kigelia africana flavonoids have been shown to be enzyme inhibitory, and extracts of Sarcocephalus latifolius demonstrated anti-oxidant and acetylcholinesterase inhibitory activity [5], [6], [7].

Phytochemicals such as flavonoids, alkaloids, phenolics, terpenoids, saponins and glycosides are known to possess many bioactive properties such as antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, anti-cholinesterase, anti-amyloid and neuroprotectant [8], [9], [10], [11], [12]. These properties are particularly pertinent to pathogenesis of AD, which is typified by oxidative stress, chronic neuroinflammation, deposition of amyloid-2 plaques, tau phosphorylation, and loss of cholinergic neurotransmission. Medications that can stabilize one or more of these pathways can slow the progression of the disease or improve symptoms.

Regions such as Central and West Africa are most biodiversified regarding medicinal flora. Climate, soils, seasons, and cultural practices vary greatly across these regions, therefore, the plant species used, the parts of plant harvested, the method of preparation, and the bioactive profiles may vary radically between these regions. Extensive surveys of African ethnomedicinal plants and functional foods demonstrate dozens of species in most plant families have been shown to have effects on Alzheimer-related phenotypes, such as balancing oxidative stress, neuroinflammation, acetylcholinesterase action, amyloid aggregation, and even improving memory in animal models [15], [16].

Despite the abundance of variety of plant species in these regions, phytochemical composition and activities in AD-related models of the majority of these plants remains undocumented. Additionally, there is scanty research on comparative studies between the Central and West African plants. Meanwhile, safety, and dosage are not well-reported too. Therefore, the review of Central and West African plants is timely not only to discover new molecules with therapeutic potential, learn about the regional diversity of phytochemistry, and find safe and effective leads to develop. This could help to address existing gaps in therapies of AD, particularly in low resource setting like the Central Africa and West Africa. Hence, the present study aimed to explore the phytochemical composition and anti-Alzheimer potential of medicinal plants from Central and West Africa.

sec.title: Methodology
sec.p: The present study is a systematic review which included biomedical researches that explored the possible treatment or management of AD using medicinal plants sourced from within any of the Central and West African countries. The group without intervention served as the control, anti-Alzheimer potential of the medicinal plants was considered as the outcome of the study. Only original articles published in English between 1987 – 2024 were searched in PubMed, Scopus, and Web of Science databases using the following search algorithm (“Alzheimer’s disease” OR “Neurodegenerative disease” OR "Nervous system diseases") AND (“Extract” OR "herbs" OR “Plant”) AND (“West Africa” OR “Central Africa” OR “Africa”). Duplicates were removed by (IMU and VA), title and abstract screening were done by two blinded authors (PM and BSB), and conflicts were then resolved by a third author (DB). Gray articles were searched for possible addition to the included articles.

Following the conclusion of the title and abstract screening, the full text screening and risk of bias assessment was done by (HI, EME, IMU, AO, COU, DI, WM, and VBA) using the Systematic Review Center for Laboratory Animal Experimentation (SYRCLE) [17] for invivo studies and Science in Risk Assessment and Policy (SCIRAP tool) as modified and used by Almeida et al. [18] was used for the assessment of the reporting quality for the in-vitro studies [19]. The result of the risk of bias assessment was presented using the ROBVIS tool. The data extraction and presentation were done by (DB and VBA). The present review were reported using the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA) [20].

sec.title: Study Selection
sec.p:
The initial search across three databases yielded a total of 351 records. Following the removal of 110 duplicates, 241 unique articles remained for title and abstract screening. After the title and abstract screening, 167 articles were excluded due to irrelevance to the study criteria, retaining 74 articles for potential inclusion. Despite extensive efforts, 2 of these reports could not be retrieved; consequently, 72 full-text articles were assessed for eligibility. From this group, 61 articles were excluded for being unrelated to the study objectives. As a result, a total of 13 articles were ultimately included for data extraction and synthesis (Fig. 1).Fig. 1
Prisma flow chart.Fig. 1

sec.title: Risk of bias assessment
sec.p:
The risk of bias was evaluated using the SYRCLE Risk of Bias tool, which covers 10 domains tailored for in vivo studies. Overall, the assessment revealed a generally low risk of bias across the included articles. However, most of the studies did not report sufficient information regarding whether caregivers or investigators were blinded to the intervention groups (Domain 5), or whether outcome assessors were blinded to the treatment groups (Domain 7), indicating some limitations in reporting on methodological rigor in these areas (Fig. 2 & 3).Fig. 2
Risk of bias for the 10 domains for the in vivo studies.Fig. 2Fig. 3
Risk of bias for the individual articles on the in vivo studies.Fig. 3

In parallel, the SciRAP tool was used to evaluate five core components: the test compound and controls, the test system, administration of the compound, data collection, and analysis. Out of the included studies, 4 studies partially met the criteria related to the description and use of test compounds and controls. In terms of the test system and how the compound was administered, 9 studies fulfilled the evaluation standards. When it came to data collection and analysis procedures, 9 studies satisfied the required criteria. Assessment of transparency in funding and declarations of competing interests revealed that only 5 articles met the criteria, whereas 5 studies failed to provide adequate disclosure in these areas (Fig. 4).Fig. 4
The reporting quality for the in-vitro studies.Fig. 4

sec.title: Study characteristics
sec.p:
Overall, 13 studies were reviewed in this current study. Most of the studies (10/13, 77 %) were conducted between 2014 and 2024. Geographically, 9 studies were conducted in Nigeria, while the rest were distributed across Ivory Coast, Benin, and São Tomé and Príncipe (Table 1). In terms of study design, 3 studies were in vivo, 9 were in vitro, and 1 was ex vivo. Leaves were the most commonly investigated plant part (9/13, 69 %), followed by roots, stems, seeds, and bark. Several studies used animal or cellular models relevant to Alzheimer’s disease, oxidative stress, or enzyme inhibition (Table 1).Table 1
Study characteristics.Table 1
S/NStudy designName of plantPlant partAnimal or CellsGender/Age (wks)Disease ModelCountryReference
1In vivoSolanum macrocarpon and Solanum nigrumLeavesDrosophila melanogaster (transgenic Aβ₄₂-expressing AD flies)(both Genders) 6–8 daysAD ModelNigeria[21]
2In vitroMacrosphyra longistylaLeavesNoneNAAD ModelNigeria[22]
3In vitroPteleopsis suberosaLeavesBacteria (S. aureus, E. faecalis, E. coli, S. typhi, C. violaceum CV026 & CV12472, P. aeruginosa PA01)NANeurodegenerative (AChE, BChE inhibition → Alzheimer’s), Metabolic (α-glucosidase, α-amylase inhibition → Diabetes), Microbial infection (antibiofilm, quorum sensing inhibition, antibacterial)Benin[23]
4Ex vivoBeta vulgaris, Persea americana and Syzygium aromaticumRoot, Leaves, Seeds and clovesWistar ratsMale / 6–8 wksFe²⁺-induced brain injuryNigeria[24]
5In vitroCitrullus lanatus (Watermelon), Cucumeropsis mannii (Egusi-itoo), Lagenaria siceraria (Bottle gourd), Citrullus colocynthis (Egusi melon)SeedsN/ANAAD ModelNigeria[25]
6In vitroAchyranthes aspera Linn.Whole aerial partsNANAAD modelIvory Coast[26]
7In vitroTithonia diversifoliaLeavesNANAAD ModelNigeria[27]
8In-vitroVoacanga africana, Tarenna nitiduloides, Sacosperma paniculatum, Psychotria principensis, Psychotria subobliquaVoacanga africana [Leaves + stems and Bark]Tarenna nitiduloides [Leaves + stems]Sacosperma paniculatum [Leaves + stems]Psychotria principensis [Leaves + stems]Psychotria subobliqua [Leaves + stem]HT22 mouse hippocampal nerve cells; MC65 human nerve cells; N9 mouse microglial cells; rat PC12 cellsNAmultiple neurotoxicity pathways relevant to AD (5 different pathways)São Tomé and Príncipe[28]
9In-vitroAngelica polymorpha Maxim, Beilschmiedia pulverulentaRoots of A. polymorpha and stem bark of B. pulverulentaNoneNAmodel via acetylcholinesterase inhibitionNigeria[29]
10In vitroBlighia sapidaStem barkNA; They mixed the Blighia sapida extract directly with isolated enzymes.NAAlzheimer’s disease-related enzyme inhibitionNigeria[24]
11In vitroCrassocephalum crepidioidesLeavesMale Wistar rats.Male; weight 190–250 gQuinolinic acid-induced oxidative stressNigeria[30]
12In vivoSolanum nigrum; Solanum macrocarponLeavesDrosophila melanogasterBoth male and female, 6–8 days oldAluminum chloride-induced memory impairment model in Drosophila, treated with donepezilNigeria[31]
13In vivoVernonia amygdalina, Morinda lucida, Senna siamea, Picralima nitida, Cnidoscolus aconitifoliusLeavesWistar ratsFemale, ∼5 monthsDichlorovinyldimethylphosphate (DDVP)-induced AlzheimerNigeria[32]
NA – Not available / Not applicable, AD – Alzheimer’s disease, Aβ₄₂ – Amyloid beta (1–42) peptide, a neurotoxic peptide linked to Alzheimer’s pathology, AChE – Acetylcholinesterase (enzyme that breaks down acetylcholine), BChE – Butyrylcholinesterase (alternative cholinesterase enzyme), α-glucosidase / α-amylase – Digestive enzymes; inhibition is linked to anti-diabetic effects, Fe²⁺ – Ferrous ion (iron II), used to induce oxidative brain injury, HT22 cells – Mouse hippocampal neuronal cell line, MC65 cells – Human neuronal cell line expressing amyloid precursor protein (APP), N9 cells – Mouse microglial cell line, PC12 cells – Rat adrenal pheochromocytoma cells used as a neuronal differentiation model, QA – Quinolinic acid, a neurotoxin used to induce oxidative stress in brain tissue, DDVP – Dichlorvos (2,2-dichlorovinyl dimethyl phosphate), an organophosphate pesticide that induces neurotoxicity.

Drosophila melanogaster (including transgenic AD models), Wistar rats, and bacteria were among the biological models employed. Cell lines such as HT22, PC12, MC65, N9, and enzyme-based systems were used in vitro. Ages and genders were specified in a few studies, with Wistar rats being male or female (6–8 weeks), and Drosophila included both sexes (Table 1). Disease models focused largely on Alzheimer’s disease, with others targeting oxidative stress, brain injury, enzyme inhibition, diabetes, and microbial infections. These studies applied both plant extracts and biochemical assays to explore therapeutic potential in neurodegenerative and metabolic disorders (Table 1).

sec.title: Phytochemical characterization and analysis
sec.p:
Of the studies reviewed, 38 % (5/13) reported using ultraviolet-visible spectroscopy (UV-Spec) to quantify total phenolic content (TPC), total flavonoid content (TFC), tannins, and antioxidant activities using assays such as DPPH (2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl), ABTS (2,2′-azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid)), FRAP (Ferric Reducing Antioxidant Power), nitric oxide scavenging, and Fe2 + chelation (Table 2). The distribution of the various methods employed in the phytochemical characterization of the key phytometabolites in plants studied in the included articles is presented in Fig. 5 (Fig. 6).Table 2
Extract and phytochemical analysis reported in the included articles.Table 2
S/NExtract or PhytocompoundUV-SpecGC-MSHPLCLC-MS/MSLC-HR-MSReference
1Polyphenols (luteolin, catechin derivatives, caffeic acid, chlorogenic acid, dihydroxycoumarin, etc.) and alkaloids (α-solanine, α-solasodine, solanidine, solamargine, demissine, solasonine).NANACaffeic acid, catechin C, dihydroxycoumarin, sorbitol, chlorogenic acid, scopolamine, α-solanine, and othersYes/ AE and BN leaf extractsNA[21]
2Crude methanol extract and fractions (n-hexane, ethyl acetate, aqueous) from Macrosphyra longistyla leavesYes/ Total phenolics: Folin–Ciocalteu at 765 nm; Total flavonoids: AlCl₃ method at 420 nm; Tannins: Vanillin–HCl at 500 nm; DPPH at 517 nm; NO scavenging at 540 nm; FRAP at 700 nm; AChE/BuChE inhibition at 412 nmYes/ decanoic acid methyl ester, 11,14-eicosadienoic acid methyl ester, linoelaidic acid, pentadecanoic acid, 2-methyl-hexadecanal, etcN/AN/AN/A[22]
3Hydro-alcoholic phenolic-rich leaf extractTotal Yes/Phenolic Content (TPC) and Total Flavonoid Content (TFC)NAYes/HPLC-DAD used; 26 phenolic standards screened; 14 phenolic compounds detected, including gallic acidN/AN/A[23]
4Flavonoid-rich extracts of crude formulations A & B (including Gallic acid, Caffeic acid, Syringic acid, Kaempferol, Quercetin, Rutin, Myricetin, p-Coumaric acid, Methyl gallate)NANAYes/ quercetin, myricetin, rutin, and kaempferolNANA[24]
5Polyphenolic fractions and oils (hexane & methanolic extracts, trans-esterified oils)Reported/ DPPH and ABTSNANANANA[25]
6Methanol extract, dichloromethane extract, ethyl acetate extract, and water infusionReported/ TPC/TFCNANANAYes/ UHPLC-HRMS[26]
7Phenolic extract of Tithonia diversifolia leaves (gallic acid, chlorogenic acid, caffeic acid, p-coumaric acid, apigenin)Total phenolics, total flavonoids, ABTS IC₅₀, Fe²⁺ chelationNAGallic acid, chlorogenic acid, caffeic acid, p-coumaric acid, apigeninNANA[27]
8Hydroethanolic extracts from plantsNANAYes (fractionation of Voacanga africana bark)NALC-HR-MS not used; ESI-HR-MS used; identified the alkaloid voacamine (MW = 704.90) as the predominant compound.[28]
913 phytochemicals were isolated from A. polymorpha: Isoimperatorin, Phellopterin, Bergapten, Xanthyletin, Cnidilin, Geijerine, (−)-3′-acetyl hamaudol, 7-demethyl suberosine, Dehydrogeijerin, (−)-hamaudol, (+)-visamminol, Divaricatol, Scopoletinwere phytoconstituents obtained from B. pulverulenta: (+)-yangambin, (+)-sesartemin, (+)-excelsin, (+)-sesamin, (+)-syringaresinol.NANANANANA[29]
10phenolic acid and flavonoids (containing gallic acid, ellagic acid, quercetin, luteolin)DPPH, ABTS, FRAPNAYesNANA[24]
11Hydrophilic extract of Crassocephalum crepidioide containing phenolic acidsPhenol/flavonoid quantificationNAPresence of seven antioxidant compoundsNANA[30]
12leaf powder of Solanum nigrum and Solanum macrocarponNANAAbundance of quercetin in both samplesNANA[31]
13Aqueous, methanol, and ethanol leaf extracts from V. amygdalina, M. lucida, S. siamea, P. nitida, C. aconitifolius.NANANANANA[32]
NA – Not applicable, UV-Spec – Ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometry, GC-MS – Gas Chromatography–Mass Spectrometry, HPLC – High-Performance Liquid Chromatography, LC-MS/MS – Liquid Chromatography–Tandem Mass Spectrometry, LC-HR-MS – Liquid Chromatography–High Resolution Mass Spectrometry, UHPLC-HRMS – Ultra-High-Performance Liquid Chromatography–High Resolution Mass Spectrometry, ESI-HR-MS – Electrospray Ionization–High Resolution Mass Spectrometry, TPC – Total Phenolic Content, TFC – Total Flavonoid Content, DPPH – 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (free radical scavenging assay), ABTS – 2,2′-azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) (free radical scavenging assay), FRAP – Ferric Reducing Antioxidant Power assay, NO scavenging – Nitric oxide radical scavenging assay, AChE – Acetylcholinesterase, BuChE – Butyrylcholinesterase, IC₅₀ – Half maximal inhibitory concentration, MW – Molecular Weight.Fig. 5
Distribution of the methods utilized for phytochemical characterization.Fig. 5Fig. 6
Chemical structures of the identified phytometabolites used in the treatment of neurodegenerative diseases as retrieved from the PubChem and SwissADME webserver’s Marvun JS ChemAxon plugin.Fig. 6

Gas Chromatography-Mass Spectrometry (GC-MS) was employed in 15 % (2/13) of the studies to identify compounds such as decanoic acid methyl ester, linoelaidic acid, and pentadecanoic acid. High-Performance Liquid Chromatography (HPLC) or HPLC with Diode-Array Detection (HPLC-DAD) was applied in 46 % (6/13) of the studies, detecting key polyphenols including caffeic acid, gallic acid, chlorogenic acid, catechin, quercetin, myricetin, and kaempferol (Table 2).

Liquid Chromatography-Tandem Mass Spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) was scarcely used, while Ultra-High-Performance Liquid Chromatography-High-Resolution Mass Spectrometry (UHPLC-HR-MS) appeared in 8 % (1/13) of the studies for profiling antioxidant-rich extracts. Additionally, Electrospray Ionization High-Resolution Mass Spectrometry (ESI-HR-MS) was used in one study to isolate voacamine, a major alkaloid from Voacanga Africana (Table 2).

Overall, flavonoids and phenolic acids (e.g., gallic acid, chlorogenic acid, caffeic acid, p-coumaric acid, luteolin) were the most frequently reported compounds. Alkaloids such as a-solanine, solasonine, solamargine, and solasodine were also identified in some plant extracts. Despite these advanced analyses, 31 % (4/13) of the studies reported only qualitative or semi-quantitative data without the use of spectrometric methods, relying mainly on phytochemical assays (Table 2).

sec.title: Key phytometabolites identified and their chemical properties
sec.p:
This review identified 16 phytometabolites with potential relevance to the treatment of AD and other neurological problems. These include Luteolin (PubChem CID: 5280445), Catechin (PubChem CID: 73160), Decanoic acid methyl ester (PubChem CID: 8181), 11,14-Eicosadienoic acid methyl ester (PubChem CID: 5282761), Caffeic acid (PubChem CID: 689043), Syringic acid (PubChem CID: 10742), Azelaic acid (PubChem CID: 2266), Undecanedioic acid (PubChem CID: 15816), Chlorogenic acid (PubChem CID: 1794427), Voacamine (PubChem CID: 11953931), Isoimperatorin (PubChem CID: 68081), Yangambin (PubChem CID: 443028), Gallic acid (PubChem CID: 370), Ellagic acid (PubChem CID: 5281855), Quercetin (PubChem CID: 5280343), and Kaempferol (PubChem CID: 5280863) (Table 3). Other chemical properties, such as the chemical formula, molecular weight (g/mol), and the Simplified Molecular Input Line Entry System (SMILES) of these key phytometabolites, are presented in Table 3. The 2D chemical structures of the reported phytometabolites used in treating neurodegenerative diseases, including AD, are presented in Fig. 5. These are provided as retrieved from the SwissADME and PubChem databases via https://www.swissadme.ch/ and https://pubchem.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/, respectively.Table 3
Chemical properties of key phytometabolites identified in the plants studied.Table 3
S/NNamePubChem CIDM.FM.W (g/mol)SMILESReference
1Luteolin5280445C15H10O6286.24C1 =CC(=C(C

PubChem CID - PubChem Compound Identifier, MF-Molecular formula, M.W (g/mol) – Molecular weight in grams/mole, SMILES -Simplified Molecular Input Line Entry System.

sec.title: Phytotherapeutics Utilized in AD and Related Pathologies
sec.p:
Various medicinal plants and phytochemicals were tested for their ability to treat Alzheimer's disease (AD) and other neurological disorders (Table 4). The leaves of Solanum macrocarpon and Solanum nigrum were polyphenols and alkaloids that alleviated AD symptoms in transgenic flies [29], [33]. The leaf extracts of Macrosphyra longistyla and Pteleopsis suberosa regulated acetylcholinesterase (AChE) and butyrylcholinesterase (BuChE) activity, thereby expressing antioxidant activities comparable to standard drugs [23]. (Table 4). Beta vulgaris, Persea americana, and Syzygium aromaticum produced formulations rich in flavonoids that also inhibited AChE and BuChE in vivo [24]. Citrullus lanatus and Cucumeropsis mannii polyphenolic seed extracts were found to be potent radical scavengers and enzyme inhibitors [25]. Achyranthes aspera aerial parts’ methanol and ethyl acetate extracts were found to inhibit neurodegeneration-related enzymes [26] (Table 4). Tithonia diversifolia leaves showed multi-target antioxidant activity [27]. Voacanga africana produced voacamine, which works in in vitro AD models [32]. Angelica polymorpha and Beilschmiedia pulverulenta yielded 18 bioactive compounds with anti-AChE potential [31]. Blighia sapida [27], Crassocephalum crepidioides (Adefegha et al., 2015), and a mixture of Vernonia amygdalina and other native plants were remarkably potent against DDVP-induced neurotoxicity (Table 4). These results revealed the multi-therapeutic potential of these botanical drugs and their phytometabolites in the treatment of AD-related pathologies.Table 4
Information on plant-based interventions and dosages used in the included studies.Table 4
S/NPlant name and partPhytocompound or extract or fractionDosageComparatorDosage of ComparatorRoute of administrationDuration of AdministrationReference
1Solanum macrocarpon, leaves and Solanum nigrum, leavespolyphenols (luteolin, catechin derivatives, caffeic acid, chlorogenic acid, dihydroxycoumarin, etc.) and alkaloids (α-solanine, α-solasodine, solanidine, solamargine, demissine, solasonine).0.1 % and 1.0 % (w/w)AD (Aβ) flies on standard diet (untreated) and wild-type control (w)N/A for untreated controlsorally14 days[21]
2Macrosphyra longistyla (leaves)Crude methanol extract and fractions (n-hexane, ethyl acetate)AChE 0.556 µg/mL (methanol), 0.914 µg/mL (ethyl acetate), 0.846 µg/mL (aqueous); BuChE 5.541 µg/mL (methanol)Eserin, Donepezil, Ascorbic acid, DDM (2,6-di-tert-butyl-4-methylphenol)AChE 0.002 µg/mL (Eserin), 0.001 µg/mL (Donepezil); Antioxidant comparators IC₅₀: 0.006 mg/mL (Ascorbic acid), 0.050 mg/mL (DDM)In vitro assaysN/A[22]
3Pteleopsis suberosa (Leaves)Hydro-alcoholic leaf extract (phenolic-rich)0.3125–2.5 mg/mL (S. aureus, E. faecalis, E. coli, S. typhi); Antioxidant IC₅₀: 15.80–41.25 μg/mL; Enzyme inhibition: AChE > 100 μg/mL, BChE 55.17 μg/mL, α-glucosidase 50.63 μg/mL, α-amylase 81.37 μg/mL, urease/tyrosinase ∼100 μg/mLGalantamine, Acarbose, BHA, α-Tocopherol, EDTA, Thiourea, Kojic acidGalantamine IC₅₀ (AChE: 5.5 μg/mL; BChE: 42.2 μg/mL); Acarbose IC₅₀ (α-glucosidase: 20.52 μg/mL; α-amylase: 32.57 μg/mL); BHA IC₅₀ (DPPH: 19.75 μg/mL; ABTS: 12.8 μg/mL); α-Tocopherol IC₅₀ (DPPH: 38.15 μg/mL; CUPRAC: 60.25 μg/mL); EDTA IC₅₀ (chelating: 5.52 μg/mL); Thiourea IC₅₀ (urease: 8.20 μg/mL); Kojic acid IC₅₀ (tyrosinase: 23.75 μg/mL)In vitro assaysNA[23]
4Beta vulgaris (leaf and root), Persea americana (seed), Syzygium aromaticum (clove)Flavonoid-rich crude formulations A and B (mixture of caffeic acid, syringic acid, gallic acid, kaempferol, quercetin, rutin, myricetin, p-coumaric acid, methyl gallate)27.950 μg/mL (AChE), 23.126 μg/mL (BChE)Galantamine27.950 μg/mL (AChE), 23.126 μg/mL (BChE)Ex vivo (rat brain tissue)NA[24]
5Citrullus lanatus (seeds), Cucumeropsis mannii (seeds), Lagenaria siceraria (seeds), Citrullus colocynthis (seeds)Polyphenolic fractions and oils (hexane & methanolic extracts, trans-esterified oils)Antioxidant (DPPH: 1.89–117.5 µg/mL; ABTS: 6.64–100.2 µg/mL); AChE inhibition (19.50–57.96 µg/mL)Ascorbic acidDPPH 222.8 µg/mL, ABTS 376.5 µg/mLIn vitro assaysNA[25]
6Achyranthes aspera Linn., aerial partsMethanol extract, dichloromethane extract, ethyl acetate extract, and water infusion0.53–0.82 mg/mL (AChE/BChE), 0.80 mg/mL (α-glucosidase), 1.90 mg/mL (tyrosinase)Galantamine for cholinesterases, Acarbose for α-glucosidase, Kojic acid for tyrosinaseNAIn vitro assaysNA[26]
7Tithonia diversifolia, LeavesPhenolic extract (containing gallic acid, chlorogenic acid, caffeic acid, p-coumaric acid, apigenin)DPPH 41.05 μg/mL, ABTS 33.51 μg/mL, Fe²⁺ chelation 38.50 μg/mL, AChE inhibition 39.27 μg/mL, BChE inhibition 35.01 μg/mLVitamin C, EDTA, ProstigmineVitamin C (standard), EDTA (standard), Prostigmine IC₅₀: AChE 50.02 μg/mL, BChE 48.56 μg/mLIn vitro assaysNA[27]
8Voacanga africana [Leaves + stems and Bark]Voacamine3–3.4 µMPositive control compound Fisetin; Negative controls included ethanol and two plant species2.5–3 µMIn vitro (cell culture)4 days in the intracellular amyloid toxicity assay; 24 h in most assays[28]
9Roots of A. polymorpha and stem bark of B. pulverulenta13 phytochemicals were isolated from A. polymorpha: Isoimperatorin, Phellopterin, Bergapten, Xanthyletin, Cnidilin, Geijerine, (−)-3′-acetyl hamaudol, 7-demethyl suberosine, Dehydrogeijerin, (−)-hamaudol, (+)-visamminol, Divaricatol, Scopoletinwere phytoconstituents obtained from B. pulverulenta: (+)-yangambin, (+)-sesartemin, (+)-excelsin, (+)-sesamin, (+)-syringaresinol.NATacrine, Galanthamine.NANA[29]
10Blighia sapida/Stem barkPhenol-rich extracts20–100 µg/mLphysostigmineNANANA[24]
11C. crepidioides/LeavesPhenolic acids and Flavonoids from Crassocephalum crepidioides leaves.125–1000 μg/mLpyridostigmine45.30 μg/mLNANA[30]
12lanum macrocarpon and Solanum nigrum/Leavesquercetin, kaempferol, rutin, gallic acid, chlorogenic acid1.0 % dietary add AE or BN + 25 µg/mL DonepezilDonepezil hydrochloride25 µg/mL (with 40 mM AlCl₃)Via feeding7 days[31]
13Vernonia amygdalina/Leavesleaf extracts of Vernonia amygdalina, Morinda lucida, Senna siamea3.3 mg/kgDDVP-induced control group0.5 mL of DDVP for 3.3 mg/kgOral14 days[32]
NA – Not applicable, AD (Aβ) – Alzheimer’s disease (Amyloid-beta model), w – Wild-type control, AChE – Acetylcholinesterase, BuChE / BChE – Butyrylcholinesterase, DDM – 2,6-di-tert-butyl-4-methylphenol (an antioxidant comparator), IC₅₀ – Half maximal inhibitory concentration, BHA – Butylated hydroxyanisole (antioxidant standard), DPPH – 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (free radical scavenging assay), ABTS – 2,2′-azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) (free radical scavenging assay), CUPRAC – Cupric reducing antioxidant capacity assay, EDTA – Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (metal chelating agent), DDVP – Dichlorvos (2,2-dichlorovinyl dimethyl phosphate; pesticide used to induce toxicity).
The various dosages, comparators with their respective dosages, routes of administration, and duration of administration of these plants and their metabolites are presented in Table 4.

sec.title: Reported mechanism of actions and AD treatment outcomes
sec.p:
The plants reviewed had neuroprotective effects, mainly in the form of acetylcholinesterase (AChE) and butyrylcholinesterase (BuChE) inhibition, antioxidant activity, and amyloid pathway regulation (Table 5). Solanum macrocarpon and S. nigrum decreased AChE and β-secretase (BACE-1), reduced β-amyloid, and enhanced behavioral outcomes in Drosophila models [29], [33]. The enzyme inhibition and radical scavenging of Macrosphyra longistyla, Pteleopsis suberosa, Tithonia diversifolia, and Crassocephalum crepidioides were potent [23], [27], [30] (Table 5). The activities of AChE, BuChE, and monoamine oxidase (MAO) were inhibited by multi-herbal and seed extracts of Beta vulgaris, Citrullus lanatus, and other plants, and they had protective effects against oxidative brain injury [24], [25]. Voacanga africana stimulated the growth of neurites and inhibited amyloid and inflammatory reactions [32] (Table 5). Other plant extracts (Achyranthes aspera, Angelica polymorpha, Blighia sapida, and Vernonia amygdalina) demonstrated good potential in preserving acetylcholine levels and enhancing antioxidant enzyme activities [26], [28], [31], [34] (Table 5). Together, these results indicate multi-targeted processes that facilitate the therapeutic effects of these plants in Alzheimer's disease (AD).Table 5
Mechanism of actions and plant-based AD treatment outcome as reported in the included studies.Table 5
S/NPlant part, extract/phytocompoundMechanism of actionTreatment outcomeReference
1Solanum macrocarpon, leaves and Solanum nigrum, leavesModulation of neural enzymes (↓AChE, ↓BACE-1), reduction of β-amyloid levels, antioxidant activity (↓ROS, ↓lipid peroxidation, ↑CAT, ↑GST, ↑total thiols)Improved survival, locomotor performance, and memory index in transgenic Drosophila melanogaster model of Alzheimer’s disease; ameliorated histopathological changes in brain tissue[21]
2Macrosphyra longistyla (leaves)Inhibition of acetylcholinesterase (AChE) and butyrylcholinesterase (BuChE); antioxidant activity (DPPH and NO radical scavenging, ferric reducing antioxidant power); correlation with phenolic and flavonoid content; GC-MS identified bioactive compounds with antimicrobial, antioxidant, anticholinesterase, anticancer, and anthelmintic effectsMethanol extract showed highest AChE inhibition (81.63 %); polar fractions had highest antioxidant and anticholinesterase activities; potential for managing Alzheimer’s disease and other neurodegenerative disorders[22]
3Pteleopsis suberosa (Leaves)Antioxidant activity, inhibition of cholinesterases, α-amylase, urease, tyrosinase; antibacterial, antibiofilm, and quorum sensing inhibitionReduced oxidative stress; inhibited key enzymes linked to Alzheimer’s disease and diabetes; suppressed bacterial growth, biofilm formation, and quorum sensing; potential to reduce microbial resistance[23]
4Beta vulgaris (leaf and root), Persea americana (seed), Syzygium aromaticum (clove)Inhibition of AChE, BChE, and MAO; antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, neuroprotective; thermodynamically stable protein-ligand interactionsTetraherbal Form A inhibited AChE, Form B inhibited BChE; both forms protected against Fe²⁺-mediated brain injury; potential neuroprotection in AD[24]
5Citrullus lanatus (seeds), Cucumeropsis mannii (seeds), Lagenaria siceraria (seeds), Citrullus colocynthis (seeds)Antioxidant activity; acetylcholinesterase (AChE) inhibitionOils and polyphenolic fractions showed strong radical scavenging; best AChE inhibition by Lagenaria siceraria polyphenolic fraction and Citrullus lanatus oil; potential neuroprotection in AD and Parkinson’s[25]
6Achyranthes aspera Linn., aerial partsAntioxidant activity; enzyme inhibition (AChE, BChE, α-glucosidase, α-amylase, tyrosinase); high total phenolic and flavonoid contents; network pharmacology predicted carbonic anhydrase II as putative targetSignificant antioxidant activity, Enzyme inhibition, potential neuroprotection, Inhibition of α-glucosidase, Neuroprotective, diuretic, cardiovascular support, anti-inflammatory[26]
7Tithonia diversifolia, LeavesAntioxidant activity; Inhibition of cholinesterase enzymes (AChE and BChE)Strong radical scavenging ability, Significant AChE inhibition and BChE inhibition, more potent than prostigmine standard; Potential neuroprotective effect against Alzheimer’s disease[27]
8Voacanga africana [Leaves + stems and Bark]Voacamine has neuroprotection through antioxidant, anti-amyloid, anti-inflammatory, and pro-neuronal differentiation pathways.Significant neuroprotection in Alzheimer’s-relevant models; outperforming positive control fisetin in several tests.[28]
9Roots of A. polymorpha and stem bark of B. pulverulentaAcetylcholinesterase (AChE) inhibition by prolonging availability of acetylcholine in the synaptic cleftPotential improvement of memory and cognitive function in Alzheimer’s disease patients by maintaining higher acetylcholine levels.[29]
10Blighia sapida/Stem barkInhibition of cholinergic enzymes and thwarting oxidative-stress-induced neurodegeneration.Significant in vitro inhibition of AChE (IC₅₀ = 125.16 μg/mL) and BChE (IC₅₀ = 230.63 μg/mL); strong antioxidant properties[34]
11C. crepidioides/Leavesinhibit acetylcholinesterase, preventing acetylcholine breakdown thus increasing brain acetylcholine levels.dose-dependent inhibition of AChE; protection against QA-induced protection against QA-induced lipid peroxidation in rat brain[30]
12lanum macrocarpon and Solanum nigrum/LeavesAMN gene upregulation (memory), Nrf2 upregulation (antioxidant defense), reduced ROS, increased catalase activity, improved AChE activityreduced oxidative stress, restored cholinergic function, enhanced memory-related gene expression; it has been observed that AE+donepezil more effective for AMN upregulation[31]
13Vernonia amygdalina/LeavesAChE inhibitioninhibited AChE activity, Improved antioxidant enzyme activities, reduced lipid peroxidation, potential neuroprotective effect[32]
AChE – Acetylcholinesterase, BChE / BuChE – Butyrylcholinesterase, BACE-1 – β-site amyloid precursor protein cleaving enzyme 1 (β-secretase 1), ROS – Reactive Oxygen Species, CAT – Catalase, GST – Glutathione S-transferase, MAO – Monoamine oxidase, Fe²⁺ – Ferrous ion (iron II), QA – Quinolinic acid (neurotoxin used to induce lipid peroxidation/oxidative damage in brain tissue), Nrf2 – Nuclear factor erythroid 2-related factor 2 (antioxidant response transcription factor), AMN gene – Amnesiac gene (memory-related gene in Drosophila melanogaster), IC₅₀ – Half maximal inhibitory concentration, ↑ (upward arrow) – Upregulation / Increase, ↓ (downward arrow) – Downregulation / Decrease.

sec.title: Central and West African medicinal plants evaluated in AD models
sec.p: The reviewed evidence highlights growing support for the neuroprotective and anti-Alzheimer’s properties of medicinal plants from Central and West Africa. Across diverse experimental models, from in vitro enzyme inhibition to in vivo Drosophila and rodent systems. It revealed that plant-derived phytochemicals modulate critical Alzheimer’s disease (AD) pathways, notably oxidative stress, acetylcholinesterase (AChE) inhibition, and neuroinflammation [5], [12], [33]. Species such as Solanum nigrum, Vernonia amygdalina, and Crassocephalum crepidioides exhibit significant neuroprotective activity, reinforcing the therapeutic potential of African flora in neurodegenerative research [28], [30].

The multitarget pharmacological actions of these extracts align with the multifactorial nature of AD. Compounds from Solanum macrocarpon and S. nigrum inhibit both AChE and butyrylcholinesterase (BChE), while Tithonia diversifolia, Macrosphyra longistyla, and Pteleopsis suberosa demonstrate strong antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties [22], [23], [34]. These mechanisms resemble those of approved AD drugs such as donepezil [29], suggesting that these plants may preserve neuronal integrity and cognitive function. The observed behavioral improvements in animal models further validate their neurobiological relevance [21], [24].

Plants from different geographical region show variation in their chemical composition [12], [35], [36]; hence, emphasizing the importance of the current review. These variations are often driven by climate and weather conditions, soil composition, light exposure, altitude, and biotic pressure [36]. Climate and weather conditions significantly influence the synthesis of phytocompounds in plants; for example, low temperatures have been reported to enhance the bio-accumulation of phytochemicals like polyphenols in some plant species [37]. Soil characteristic (such as pH, mineral availability, and organic matter) play a crucial role in shaping phytochemical biosynthesis [37]. When soils are rich in nutrients, plants tend to accumulate higher levels of specific compounds, as they have access to the essential resources required for synthesis. Phytochemical composition exhibits distinct patterns along elevation gradients [38]. At lower tropical elevations, plant species generally display greater interspecific diversity, a trait that enhances resilience against complex biotic pressures such as diverse herbivore communities [37]. In contrast, species inhabiting subalpine and alpine environments often demonstrate reduced interspecific variation but synthesize targeted compounds that enable them to withstand abiotic stressors, including elevated ultraviolet radiation and pronounced temperature fluctuations.

Regional variation in bioactivity, partly due to extraction methods, plant parts, and climatic factors, highlights the need for chemotaxonomic and metabolomic standardization [13], [14]. Despite the promise, most studies remain preliminary, limited by short-term models, small sample sizes, and insufficient pharmacokinetic or toxicological validation [22], [26]. Moreover, few assess blood–brain barrier permeability or define standardized dosages [27], [31].

sec.title: Phytochemical basis of neuroprotective activity in African Medicinal plants
sec.p: Phytochemical analyses reveal that Central and West African medicinal plants investigated for Alzheimer’s disease (AD) are consistently rich in polyphenols, flavonoids, and alkaloids (especially quercetin, gallic acid, chlorogenic acid, caffeic acid, and rutin), which exhibit antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and cholinesterase-inhibitory properties [21], [27], [30]. Extracts from Solanum nigrum, Macrosphyra longistyla, Tithonia diversifolia, and Crassocephalum crepidioides contained these bioactives, supporting their ethnomedical use for memory enhancement [22], [29]. The presence of steroidal and indole alkaloids such as solanine, solasodine, and voacamine further suggests potential modulation of cholinergic signaling and neuroinflammation [21], [32].

The multitarget pharmacology of these phytochemicals is particularly relevant for AD’s multifactorial pathogenesis. Flavonoids and phenolic acids enhance antioxidant defense through Nrf2/ARE activation, suppress NF-κB-mediated inflammation, and inhibit amyloid-β aggregation, while alkaloids and terpenoids contribute to AChE inhibition [8], [27]. Such broad-spectrum effects strengthen the biochemical rationale for traditional cognitive remedies [5], [12].

However, methodological variability ranging from advanced HPLC or LC–HR-MS analyses to less precise spectrophotometric assays limits comparability across studies [22], [23]. Environmental and cultural factors, including soil and harvest conditions, further affect phytochemical profiles, emphasizing the need for standardized chemotaxonomic and metabolomic protocols across regions [13], [14]. The scarcity of pharmacokinetic, toxicity, and bioavailability data also constrains translational potential.

sec.title: Neuroprotective phytometabolites underpinning Anti-Alzheimer’s potential of African medicinal plants
sec.p: The identified phytometabolites across African medicinal plants reveal a chemically diverse yet functionally convergent profile rich in polyphenols, flavonoids, and phenolic acids. These were key compounds with antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and cholinesterase-inhibitory actions targeting core Alzheimer’s disease (AD) mechanisms [21], [27], [30]. Among them, flavonoids such as luteolin, quercetin, kaempferol, and catechin exhibit multitarget neuroprotection through radical scavenging, metal chelation, and regulation of Nrf2 and NF-κB signaling [29]. Luteolin and quercetin additionally inhibit AChE and modulate tau phosphorylation, while catechin supports synaptic plasticity and neuronal survival [21].

Phenolic acids, including caffeic, chlorogenic, gallic, syringic, and ellagic acids enhance endogenous antioxidant defenses and reduce lipid peroxidation [12], [24]. Caffeic and chlorogenic acids, found in Tithonia diversifolia and Crassocephalum crepidioides, suppress neuroinflammation and protect dopaminergic neurons, whereas gallic acid shows anti-amyloid and metal-chelating activity [30]. Fatty acid derivatives like decanoic and eicosadienoic acid esters from Macrosphyra longistyla maintain membrane integrity, and alkaloids such as voacamine from Voacanga africana modulate cholinergic signaling and inflammation [22], [32].

Other metabolites include coumarins and lignans such as isoimperatorin and yangambin. These phytometabolites demonstrate AChE inhibition and calcium-channel modulation, mitigating synaptic dysfunction [31]. Structurally, most compounds have moderate molecular weights (170–350 g/mol) and polarity that favor solubility and potential blood–brain barrier (BBB) permeability, while larger alkaloids like voacamine may serve as scaffolds for optimized derivatives [32].

Collectively, these compounds exhibit biochemical synergy: flavonoids and phenolic acids primarily counter oxidative and inflammatory damage, while alkaloids and coumarins regulate neurotransmission and enzyme inhibition. This multitarget interplay reflects AD’s multifactorial nature and supports observed neuroprotective outcomes in preclinical studies [21], [24].

sec.title: Reported Anti-Alzheimer’s interventions and dosing paradigms in Central and West Africa
sec.p: Across the reviewed studies, three primary preclinical intervention models emerged as dominant: oral or dietary administration of whole plant extracts in Drosophila (Transgenic flies expressing human Amyloid Precursor Protein and b-secretase were administered pulverised leaf samples (0.1 and 1.0 %) in their diets for 14 days) and rodent models, enzyme- and cell-based IC₅₀ assays, and short-term mechanistic cell studies. These complementary approaches collectively demonstrate the neuroprotective, antioxidant, and cholinergic-modulating potential of African medicinal plants against Alzheimer’s disease (AD)-related pathologies. Dietary inclusion of Solanum macrocarpon/nigrum extracts (0.1–1.0 % w/w) and oral administration of Vernonia amygdalina (∼3.3 mg/kg for 14 days) produced measurable behavioral and neurochemical improvements in Drosophila and rodent models, respectively [21], [29], reflecting traditional phytotherapeutic use but revealing the need for improved pharmacological standardization

In vitro assays further revealed that phenolic-rich extracts from species such as Macrosphyra longistyla, Pteleopsis suberosa, and Tithonia diversifolia achieved mid-µg/mL cholinesterase inhibition and antioxidant activity, while alkaloid and coumarin scaffold such as voacamine from Voacanga africana and isoimperatorin from Angelica polymorpha, demonstrated stronger, low-micromolar efficacy in amyloid-related and AChE-inhibition models [31], [32]. Similarly, compounds like yangambin from Beilschmiedia pulverulenta exhibited inhibition comparable to tacrine or galantamine [31]. Recurrent phytochemicals, including gallic, caffeic, and chlorogenic acids, together with flavonoids such as quercetin and kaempferol, were identified as key bioactive agents underpinning synergistic antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and cholinergic effects [27], [30].

These findings underscore that repeated oral or dietary administration over 7–14 days more accurately mimics clinical use than acute IC₅₀ assays, offering better insights into pharmacodynamics and safety. However, many studies lack data on pharmacokinetics, blood–brain barrier (BBB) penetration, and metabolism, limiting translational interpretation. Comparators such as galantamine, donepezil, and ascorbate were useful in establishing reference potency [23], [27], revealing that while plant extracts often exhibit lower intensity, they provide a broader multitarget neuroprotective effect. Collectively, these results highlight the promise of African phytochemicals as cost-effective, multifunctional therapeutic leads for AD management and reinforce the need for rigorous standardization, pharmacokinetic profiling, and formulation optimization to advance them toward clinical application.

sec.title: Mechanistic Pathways and Therapeutic Outcomes of African Plant-Based Interventions in Alzheimer’s Disease
sec.p: Evidence across studies shows that African medicinal plants act through multitarget mechanisms relevant to Alzheimer’s disease (AD), including cholinesterase inhibition, antioxidant (Nrf2-mediated) defense, anti-amyloid activity, and anti-inflammatory modulation. Extracts of Solanum nigrum and S. macrocarpon lowered AChE and BACE-1, reduced oxidative stress, and improved survival, memory, and locomotion in Drosophila AD models [29], [33]. Comparable cholinergic and antioxidant benefits were observed in Vernonia amygdalina, Crassocephalum crepidioides, and Tithonia diversifolia, aligning biochemical modulation with neuroprotection in vitro and in vivo [27], [28], [30].

Cholinesterase inhibition and antioxidant synergy emerged as the most consistent mechanistic pairing. Polar extracts from Macrosphyra longistyla and Pteleopsis suberosa inhibited AChE/BChE and scavenged radicals, while Beta vulgaris, Persea americana, and Syzygium aromaticum formulations added MAO inhibition and neuroprotection against Fe²⁺-induced damage [23], [24]. At the single-compound level, voacamine from Voacanga africana demonstrated low-µM activity and broad neuroprotection across amyloid and oxidative pathways [32], whereas coumarins and lignans from Angelica polymorpha and Beilschmiedia pulverulenta reinforced AChE inhibition [31].

Therapeutically, extracts that suppressed AChE/BChE and oxidative damage improved cognitive and neurobehavioral outcomes, reduced lipid peroxidation, and protected neuronal cells; co-administration with donepezil sometimes enhanced memory-related gene expression [24], [33]. Oils and phenolic fractions from Cucumeropsis, Citrullus, and Lagenaria species further stabilized membranes and redox homeostasis despite moderate AChE inhibition [24], [25].

However, limitations persist: most assays are short-term, lack pharmacokinetic or BBB data, and employ heterogeneous extract preparations with limited standardization or chronic safety evaluation [26]. Anti-amyloid activity is well supported in invertebrate and cell models but remains underexplored in mammalian systems [32].

sec.title: Future research directions
sec.p: The number of articles included in the present study though relatively small, but address fundamental issues on the benefit of medicinal plants in the potential management of Alzheimer; hence, lay a good foundation for further study in this region.

Future investigations should adopt a multidisciplinary framework that integrates phytochemistry, neuropharmacology, and behavioral neuroscience to identify bioactive leads through bioassay-guided fractionation and metabolomic profiling. Long-term in vivo studies, particularly in transgenic or aged Alzheimer’s disease (AD) models, are essential to evaluate amyloid-β and tau modulation while elucidating molecular pathways such as Nrf2, NF-κB, and cholinergic signaling [24], [32]. Comparative analyses of Central and West African chemotypes, coupled with comprehensive toxicological and pharmacokinetic screening, should be conducted to map regional variations in bioactive compound profiles, safety, and efficacy. To strengthen translational potential, future studies should establish continental phytochemical repositories and spectral libraries for African medicinal plants to facilitate cross-laboratory compound identification and dereplication. Additionally, standardized protocols for plant cultivation, harvesting, and extraction, anchored in Good Agricultural and Collection Practices (GACP) and green chemistry principles, should be developed to ensure consistency, sustainability, and reproducibility.

Moreover, incorporating high-throughput screening platforms and AI-assisted network pharmacology will enhance the discovery of multitarget interactions relevant to oxidative stress, neuroinflammation, and synaptic plasticity. Systems biology models should be applied to integrate multi-omics data, metabolomics, transcriptomics, and proteomics, to identify synergistic compound combinations with disease-modifying potential. Strategic north–south and intra-African collaborations are also needed to harmonize analytical infrastructure, share compound libraries, and build research capacity in neurodegenerative disease studies across the continent.

Future research should further prioritize quantitative LC–MS/MS metabolomics, molecular docking, and blood–brain barrier (BBB) permeability assessments to elucidate multi-target interactions and bioavailability of active compounds. Evaluation of chronic safety profiles, standardized extraction and formulation methods, and reproducible dosing metrics will improve translational validity and facilitate regulatory compliance.

A dual-track approach is recommended, first, optimizing standardized phenolic and flavonoid fractions to enhance potency, reproducibility, and scalability through advanced formulation techniques such as nanoencapsulation and lipid-based delivery systems; and second, advancing defined alkaloid and coumarin scaffolds, including voacamine and isoimperatorin, through rigorous pharmacokinetic, BBB, and safety validation pipelines [27], [31], [32]. Integration of donepezil or galantamine comparators in preclinical models is also advised to maintain interpretability, benchmark pharmacodynamic efficacy, and align findings with existing therapeutic standards. Finally, establishing multi-center data repositories for pharmacological and toxicological outcomes will promote reproducibility, enable meta-analytical synthesis, and lay the groundwork for early-phase clinical translation of phytotherapeutics in Central and West Africa.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Central and West African medicinal plants represent a rich reservoir of multifunctional neuroprotective metabolites capable of targeting diverse AD pathways. Key phytochemical classes include flavonoids (luteolin, quercetin, kaempferol), phenolic acids (gallic, caffeic, chlorogenic), and alkaloids (voacamine). These constitute potent, multitarget therapeutic candidates offering affordable and easily accessible intervention alternatives for neurodegeneration, especially in resource-limited settings in the various communities in Central and Western Africa.

Persistent gaps remain, however, including heterogeneous extraction protocols, inconsistent dose normalization, and limited pharmacokinetic/BBB data. Addressing these will be pivotal for clinical translation. To unravel the neuroprotective and other therapeutic potentials of African phytometabolites, harmonized comparators, validated endpoints, and multidisciplinary collaborations linking ethnobotany, chemistry, and neuroscience are required. With rigorous optimization, these botanical candidates could evolve from promising IC₅₀ profiles to standardized, orally viable anti-Alzheimer’s agents. Ultimately, integrating phytochemistry with neuropharmacology and formulation science will provide a viable pathway for developing clinically translatable botanical therapeutics, bridging traditional African knowledge with contemporary biomedical innovation.

sec.title: CRediT authorship contribution statement
sec.p: Augustin Oviosun: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Usman Ibe Michael: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Darius Benimana: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Hyppolyte Iradukunda: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Comfort Ojochenemi Usman: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Wusa Makena: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. David Ikwuka: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Ekom Monday Etukudo: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Ben Shalom Byishimo: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Protais Mukunzi: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Victor Bassey Archibong: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.

sec.title: Funding
sec.p: The study did not receive any funding

sec.title: Declaration of Competing Interest
sec.p: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sec.title: Data availability
sec.p: Data will be made available on request.
